description: Kawhi Leonard's strategic rest games during the 2018-19 season sparked a league-wide debate on load management. Fans initially opposed star players sitting out, but Leonard's championship run with the Raptors shifted opinion. Pro-load management support grew from ~20% to ~35% as evidence of its effectiveness accumulated.

key_events.description: Kawhi Leonard sits out 22 regular season games. Fans boo in arenas. TNT's Charles Barkley calls it 'disrespectful to paying fans'.
Adam Silver publicly criticizes load management. NBA sends memo to teams warning about healthy players sitting out nationally televised games.
Raptors reach NBA Finals. Narrative shifts — Leonard looks fresh while opponents show fatigue. Sports medicine professionals defend the practice.
Leonard wins Finals MVP and championship. 'Load management works' becomes mainstream take. Other teams adopt similar strategies.
2019-20 preseason: multiple stars adopt load management. Debate normalizes. NBA introduces new rules limiting back-to-back rest but accepts the concept.

calibration_notes: Gradual opinion shift driven by results-based evidence. Initial strong opposition (~60%) rooted in 'fans pay to see stars play' sentiment. Shift accelerated by Kawhi's championship — concrete proof that rest strategies work. Opposition remained majority but softened significantly. Tests: slow persuasion through demonstrated outcomes, expert vs. fan opinion divergence, sports culture change.
